ERRAND: Find the elevation (height above sea level) of Mount Everest using Wikipedia. Report the

figure in meters and feet. Be careful to report the ELEVATION specifically — not prominence,

isolation, or any other number on the page.

When done, report back exactly these lines:

VERDICT: PASS / PARTIAL / FAIL

RESULT: the elevation figure

EVIDENCE: how you verified it is the elevation from the page

LESSON: what broke / what you'd do differently / "clean run"

Claude is AI and can make mistakes. Please double-check responses.
Claude's plan

Allow actions on these sites

en.wikipedia.org

Approach to follow

1
Navigate to the Mount Everest Wikipedia article
2
Locate the infobox and identify the 'Elevation' field specifically (distinguishing it from prominence/isolation)
3
Read the elevation value in both meters and feet
4
Verify the figure is labeled as elevation on the page
5
Report VERDICT, RESULT, EVIDENCE, and LESSON
Approve plan
Make changes
Ctrl +

Claude will only use the sites listed. You'll be asked before accessing anything else.

VERDICT: PASS / PARTIAL / FAIL

LESSON: what broke / what you'd do differently / "clean run"

13 steps
13 steps
VERDICT: PASS
RESULT: Elevation of Mount Everest = 8,848.86 meters (29,031.7 feet)
EVIDENCE: On the Mount Everest Wikipedia article (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest), the infobox under the "Highest point" section has a row explicitly labeled "Elevation" showing "8,848.86 m (29,031.7 ft)". I zoomed in to confirm the label-to-value pairing and verified it is distinct from the adjacent "Prominence" row (which only states "Ranked 1st / Special definition for Everest") and the "Isolation" row (which states "n/a"). So the figure is specifically the elevation, not prominence or isolation.
LESSON: Clean run. The only minor friction was that the find tool failed because the full page DOM exceeded the token limit, but a screenshot plus a targeted zoom on the infobox was a faster and more reliable way to read and verify the specific labeled field anyway. Note: the "8,848.86 m" figure is the 2020 China–Nepal joint re-survey value reflected on the page; the older commonly cited figure was 8,848 m (29,029 ft).

13 steps
13 steps

I'll plan this out before executing.

Created a plan
Navigate to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/...
Find: "Elevation field in the infobox"

The page may still be loading. Let me take a screenshot and read the page.

Batch — 2/2 actions
Navigate to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/...
Take screenshot
Tool: browser_batch

The page loaded and I can see the infobox. There's an "Elevation" field at the bottom showing "8,848.86 m (29,031.7 ft)". Let me find that element to verify it precisely.

Find: "Elevation row value in the inf..."

Let me zoom into the elevation row to read it precisely.

Scroll down

Now I can clearly see the infobox under "Highest point" with distinct rows. Let me zoom in to confirm the exact figures and that the Elevation row is correctly distinguished from Prominence and Isolation.

Zoom

The zoom confirms it clearly. The infobox row explicitly labeled "Elevation" shows 8,848.86 m (29,031.7 ft), distinct from the "Prominence" row (which just says "Ranked 1st / Special definition for Everest") and the "Isolation" row (which says "n/a").

Done
VERDICT: PASS
RESULT: Elevation of Mount Everest = 8,848.86 meters (29,031.7 feet)
EVIDENCE: On the Mount Everest Wikipedia article (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest), the infobox under the "Highest point" section has a row explicitly labeled "Elevation" showing "8,848.86 m (29,031.7 ft)". I zoomed in to confirm the label-to-value pairing and verified it is distinct from the adjacent "Prominence" row (which only states "Ranked 1st / Special definition for Everest") and the "Isolation" row (which states "n/a"). So the figure is specifically the elevation, not prominence or isolation.
LESSON: Clean run. The only minor friction was that the find tool failed because the full page DOM exceeded the token limit, but a screenshot plus a targeted zoom on the infobox was a faster and more reliable way to read and verify the specific labeled field anyway. Note: the "8,848.86 m" figure is the 2020 China–Nepal joint re-survey value reflected on the page; the older commonly cited figure was 8,848 m (29,029 ft).
